coredata.dc:title: Multicolor fluorescent of 1D PoPD for LEDs
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Kunpeng
Bai, Yiran
Zhu, Guisheng
Xu, Huarui
Wei, Tingting
Zhao, Yunyun
Shang, Fei
Xu, Chunyu
Jiang, Li
Han, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor fluorescent materials are gaining traction in photoelectric applications due to their ability to achieve various color patterns. Aromatic amines are highly promising candidates for their rigid benzene ring and amine groups. However, achieving regulation of fluorescence emission wavelength remains elusive. In this work, we proposed a novel OH−/-OH synergistic scheme which successfully achieved the fluorescence emission wavelength shift of PoPD through deprotonation blue-shift and hydrogen bond effect red-shift. We obtained green, yellow, and red fluorescent 1D materials on a micro/nano scale. Interestingly, TEM confirmed that the fiber-structured PoPD transforms from solid fibers to hollow tubes after treatment with –OH. The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) were calculated as 16.1% (green), 15.4% (yellow) and 9.4% (red), respectively. The lifetimes were measured as 4.15 ns (green), 2.08 ns (yellow) and 1.32 ns (red), respectively. Furthermore, we fabricated multicolor LEDs. As expected, green, yellow and red 1D fluorescent materials emitted green, yellow and red light in the dark field. In particular, we achieved white emitting light by fabricating green fibers to a blue chip. This study investigates an important OH−/-OH synergistic method for controlling the fluorescence emission wavelength and could serve as a new strategy for preparing multicolored materials on 1D scale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PoPD
Multicolor fluorescence
OH−/-OH synergistic
LEDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of multicolored PoPD 1D fluorescent materials
Fabrication and the characteristics of multicolor LEDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (Grant No. AD21075022 , AD21220029 ); Middle-aged and Young Teachers' Basic Ability Promotion Project of Guangxi (Grant No. 2021KY0215 ); Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials Foundation (Grant No. 211016-Z ); Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (Grant No. GUTQDJJ2019011 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U21A2065 ); Science and Technology Major Project of Guangxi (Grant No. AA21077018 , AB20297053 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence properties of multicolor fluorescent 1D PoPD. a)-c) Fluorescence microscopy images of g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD. d) Photographs of multicolor fluorescent of 1D PoPD in an ultraviolet camera obscura (above for side view, below for front view). e) Fluorescence spectrum of g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD. f) Lifetime of g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD. Scale bar: a) 50 μm, b) 50 μm, c) 50 μm.

Morphology characterization of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD. a)-b) SEM images of o-PoPD fibers at low magnification and high magnification. c) EDS mapping of carbon and nitrogen for o-PoPD fibers. d)-e) SEM images of g-PoPD at low magnification and high magnification. f) EDS mapping of carbon and nitrogen for g-PoPD. g)-j) TEM images of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD (g-o-PoPD, h-g-PoPD, i-y-PoPD, j-r-PoPD). Scale bar: a) 500 μm, b) 10 μm, d) 500 μm, e) 10 μm, g) 1 μm, h) 1 μm, i) 1 μm, j) 1 μm.
a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD. b) FT-IR spectrogram of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD. c) Ion resistance data of fluorescent 1D PoPD.

XPS characterization of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD. a)-c) High-resolution spectra of the deconvoluted peaks for C 1s, N 1s and O 1s in o-PoPD. d)-f) High-resolution spectra of the deconvoluted peaks of C 1s in g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD. g) The proportion of different types of C, N, O atoms in o-PoPD, g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD. h) Percentages of C, N, O, N/C and O/C atomic ratios of o-PoPD, g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD.
Mechanism analysis of multicolor fluorescent 1D PoPD.

Application of multicolor LEDs. a)-d) Optical photographs of multicolor LEDs and white LEDs. e)-h) Emission spectra of g-PoPD, y-PoPD, r-PoPD LEDs and white LEDs. i) CIE color coordinates of g-PoPD, y-PoPD, r-PoPD LEDs and white LEDs.

head.ce:title: Multicolor fluorescent of 1D PoPD for LEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Engineering Research Center of Electronic Information Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor fluorescent materials are gaining traction in photoelectric applications due to their ability to achieve various color patterns. Aromatic amines are highly promising candidates for their rigid benzene ring and amine groups. However, achieving regulation of fluorescence emission wavelength remains elusive. In this work, we proposed a novel OH−/-OH synergistic scheme which successfully achieved the fluorescence emission wavelength shift of PoPD through deprotonation blue-shift and hydrogen bond effect red-shift. We obtained green, yellow, and red fluorescent 1D materials on a micro/nano scale. Interestingly, TEM confirmed that the fiber-structured PoPD transforms from solid fibers to hollow tubes after treatment with –OH. The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) were calculated as 16.1% (green), 15.4% (yellow) and 9.4% (red), respectively. The lifetimes were measured as 4.15 ns (green), 2.08 ns (yellow) and 1.32 ns (red), respectively. Furthermore, we fabricated multicolor LEDs. As expected, green, yellow and red 1D fluorescent materials emitted green, yellow and red light in the dark field. In particular, we achieved white emitting light by fabricating green fibers to a blue chip. This study investigates an important OH−/-OH synergistic method for controlling the fluorescence emission wavelength and could serve as a new strategy for preparing multicolored materials on 1D scale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The issue of fluorescence material has fascinated mankind due to their significant impact on biomedical imaging, anti-counterfeiting, photoelectric sensors, catalysts and other fields [1–5]. Currently, the main types of fluorescent materials include semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs), rare-earth fluorescent materials, and organic fluorescent dyes [6–10]. In order to achieve a general regularity of the fluorescence mechanism based on the specific behavior of individuals, various hypotheses have been employed to explain the fluorescent phenomenon, which include but not limited to quantum size effect, crosslink-enhanced emission effect, surface functional group effect [11–14]. However, there is still a scarcity of universally recognized mechanism for the actual fluorescence mechanism of many fluorescent materials, especially one-dimensional (1D) nanofluorescent material [15–19].

It is always a great challenge in the field of fluorescent materials research to achieve multiple emission wavelengths through a single material to form multicolor [20–23]. Additionally, low toxicity, facile preparation method, high fluorescence color purity, and strong bleaching resistance are also reasonable requirements for excellent fluorescent materials [24–26]. Taking the above factors into consideration, we aim to establish an approach for obtain novel micro/nano-sized multicolor fluorescent 1D materials through a facile chemical treatment method at room temperature. In 2015, Lin et al. successfully synthesized multicolor CDs with red, green, and bule fluorescence from o-phenylenediamine(oPD), m-phenylenediamine (mPD), and p-phenylenediamine (pPD) precursors, respectively [27]. This work demonstrated that multicolor fluorescence can be achieved by controlling the molecular structure of aromatic amines.

The rigid structure of the benzene ring in aromatic amines promotes conjugation, leading to fluorescence [28–30].Additionally, the presence of amine groups offers an effective active site for subsequent group modification and doping [31–33]. Due to these two reasons, aromatic amines are highly promising candidates for achieving multicolor 1D fluorescent materials. The most critical technology for controlling the wavelength of fluorescence emission is the mechanism behind red-shift and blue-shift of fluorescence emission wavelength. Sun et al. reported that the evident red-shift was caused by hydrogen bonding between –OH and the amino groups [34]. Ostrikov and co-workers announced that the deprotonation process changed the molecular state of CDs, resulting in a blue-shift [35]. Although there are few reports on preparing multicolor 1D fluorescent materials using aromatic amines, numerous reports on preparing multicolor CDs using aromatic amines confirm the feasibility of preparing multicolor 1D fluorescent materials using aromatic amines from this perspective [36–38].

The structure of oPD involves two adjacent amine groups on the benzene ring, which satisfies the structural requirements for multicolor 1D fluorescent materials. Therefore, we have chosen poly o-phenylenediamine (PoPD) fibers as our research object for colorful fluorescent fibers [39,40]. In order to obtain multicolor fluorescent fibers, two important problems need to be solved. Firstly, it is necessary to prepare PoPD fibers with a uniform morphology. The common method for preparing PoPD fiber involves oxidizing oPD monomers into PoPD oligomers with 2, 3-aminophenazine structure and then self-assembling them under non-covalent bond forces to obtain PoPD fibers [41,42]. The second step is to regulate the surface functional groups and defects of PoPD fibers in order to obtained multicolor PoPD 1D fluorescent materials.

Inspired by the polychromatic polyphenylene diamine fluorescent quantum dots, we investigated an ingenious OH−/-OH synergistic strategy to prepare micro/nano-sized PoPD multicolor 1D fluorescent materials that exhibit green, yellow and red emission at room temperature. The regulation of OH− ions leads to deprotonation and subsequent doping of oxygen-containing functional groups, resulting in a blue-shift in the emission wavelength. Additionally, the presence of –OH and –NH2 groups facilitates hydrogen bonding, which induces a chain polymerization effect and consequently causes a red-shift in the emission wavelength. Novelty, as expected, OH− treated PoPD fibers emit green fluorescence. The synergy of OH−/-OH resulted in yellow fluorescence, while –OH treated PoPD fibers emit red fluorescence. The purpose of this study is to establish a simple and effective method for achieving controllable regulation of the wavelength of fluorescence emission, which will contribute to nanoelectronics and nanophotonic applications. According to the results of the study, the initial realization of the study's purpose has been achieved.

Potassium bromide (spectral purity), oPD (analytic reagent, AR) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used as received without further purification. Sodium hydroxide (AR), sodium chloride (AR), hydrogen peroxide (30%), ethyl alcohol was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Millipore Milli-Q water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm−1, which obtained from Heal Force ultra-pure water system, was used for solution preparation in all experiments. Water used in the preparation of the oPD solution needs to be deoxygenated by nitrogen about 20 min. UV chips (365 nm) and blue chips (460 nm) were purchased form Suzhou Uking Photoelectric Technology Co., Ltd.

PoPD fibers were synthesized via a facile chemical oxidation method. Typically, 1 ml of 0.12 M HCl aqueous solution was added to 1 ml of 0.1 M fresh oPD aqueous solution, followed by the addition of 0.5 ml of 6.18 M NaCl aqueous solution. Mixed the solution well on a shaker. Next, add 0.5 ml of 30% H2O2 solution quickly, and the solution mixed well on a shaker again for another 60 s. After completing these steps, the solution was left undisturbed for 24 h to allow full growth of PoPD fibers. During this process, the colorless and transparent solution gradually turned dark brown and accompanied by a significant amount of brown-yellow precipitates formation. The precipitates were separated from the solution by high-speed centrifugation (10000 r min−1, 10 min). Then, the precipitates were washed three times with deionized water before being dried at 60 °C in an oven for 24 h and stored at room temperature for further processing. Please note that the precipitation process should not be violently disturbed, as it may result in the destruction of the 1D structure of the fibers. The sample is referred to as original PoPD (o-PoPD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of multicolored PoPD 1D fluorescent materials
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of green-PoPD (g-PoPD). Add 7.5 mg of PoPD fibers to 1 ml of a 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution, followed by gentle shaking for 1 h. The resulting precipitates was separated from the solution by high-speed centrifugation (10000 r min−1, 10 min), and then washed once with deionized water.

Preparation of yellow-PoPD (y-PoPD). The preparation process is similar to g-PoPD, but replacing 1 ml of 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution with a solution composed of 0.1 M NaOH and ethyl alcohol in a volume ratio of 2:1.
Preparation of red-PoPD (r-PoPD). The preparation process is similar to g-PoPD, but replacing 1 ml of 0.1 M NaOH aqueous solution to 1 ml ethyl alcohol and the washing process is omitted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and the characteristics of multicolor LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: Add organic silicone AB glue into the centrifuge tube (with a mass ratio of 1:1 for A glue and B glue). Stir for 2–5 min, then add a small amount of fluorescent PoPD materials into AB glue and stir until evenly mixed. Next, pour the mixture on the LED chips and place them in an oven at 90 °C to take shape.

Fluorescence microscope photographs were obtained using a Nikon Eclipse 80i fluorescence microscope. Photographs of PoPD fluorescent fibers in ultraviolet camera obscura were taken with a ZF-7 camera chamber UV analyzer (Mingren, China). The fluorescence (FL) spectrum and lifetime data came from a Quanta Master 8000 (HORIBA, America) steady state and lifetime spectrofluorometer. The SEM experiments were performed using a Gemini SEM 300 (Zeiss, England) field emission scanning electron microscope. TEM images were obtained from a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) field emission transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra data were performed using a TENSOR27 (NETZSCH, Germany) infrared spectrometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a SmartLab (Rigaku, Japan) X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) profiles were recorded on a Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher, America).

The QYs of the g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD were determined using Rhodamine B as a reference (ethanol, QYs = 85%). PoPD Fluorescent materials were uniformly dispersed in aqueous solution. QYs of the PoPD Fluorescent materials were calculated according formula (1):
(1)
Φ

Φ—QYs
Grad—Specific value of fluorescence intensity to UV–vis intensity.
η—Refractive index
st—Rhodamine B
sm—PoPD fluorescent materials
smds—PoPD fluorescent materials dissolve in solution

Fig. 1 a)-c) presents representative fluorescence microscopy images of g-PoPD, y-PoPD, and r-PoPD. After solution treatment, the fluorescent materials of PoPD still maintain their basic 1D structure. The multicolor fluorescent 1D PoPD exhibits typical green, yellow and red colors. We further examined the monochromatic fluorescence intensity of these fluorescent 1D PoPD samples, and simulation results showed a perfect match with the fluorescence microscopy images (Figure S 1 a-c). Subsequently, to observe the macroscopic fluorescence phenomenon of 1D PoPD, we placed the 1D PoPD fluorescent materials in an ultraviolet camera obscura (Fig. 1 d). By adjusting the molar ratio of OH−/-OH (NaOH solution provide OH−, C2H5OH provide –OH, and the ratio of OH−/-OH was controlled by the mole ratio of NaOH and C2H5OH), we observed a change in the fluorescence color of originally non-luminous 1D PoPD from green to yellow and then to red. The emission wavelength for green, yellow, and red fluorescent 1D PoPD is determined by the fluorescence spectrum. The emission wavelengths of green, yellow, and red fluorescent 1D PoPD are shown in Fig. 1 e, corresponding to 540.8 nm, 577.2 nm and 637.3 nm, respectively. These wavelengths perfectly match the visible color bands. As expected, we were able to control the fluorescence emission wavelength through a solution treatment method and obtain representative materials for green, yellow, and red fluorescence. The fluorescence quantum yield was calculated as follows: 16.1% (green), 15.4% (yellow), and 9.4% (red). Additionally, the lifetime of these fluorescent materials was measured at 4.15 ns (green), 2.08 ns (yellow), and 1.32 ns (red) as shown in Fig. 1 f.

To further confirm the morphology of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD, SEM and TEM were employed and the results are shown in Fig. 2 and Figure S 2. As seen in Fig. 2 a, a large number of homogeneous PoPD fibers were obtained through chemical oxidation. The length-to-diameter ratio of the PoPD fibers is over 100. Upon high magnification (Fig. 2 b), we observed minimal surface defects and a cross-section structure of the PoPD fiber that deviates from a typical circular shape but instead exhibits a novel rectangular square shape. EDS mapping verifies that the main elements C and N are evenly distributed on the fibers (Fig. 2 c). Fig. 2d and e, Figure S 2 a-b and Figure S 2 d-e presents representative fluorescent 1D PoPD structures in green, yellow, and red respectively. After solution treatment, the 1D structure of PoPD is still maintained but there are numerous surface defects. EDS mapping verify that the main elements C and N are evenly distributed on the fibers. Figure S 2 e highlights a hollow structure which was broken under the impact of the electron beam; we assume that during ethanol treatment, PoPD fibers self-assemble to form a hollow tubular structure. To confirm our hypothesis, TEM was conducted and the images are shown in Fig. 2 g-j. Compared with PoPD fibers, there is a significant increase in surface defects for green and yellow fluorescent fibers. Fig. 2 j confirms our conjecture that red fluorescent fibers have a hollow tubular structure on account of TEM images display typical tubular structures with bright middles and dark edges.

To gain insight into the crystalline structure of the PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD, XRD pattern was performed and the results are shown in Figure S 3. The PoPD fibers exhibit a series of sharp peaks in the region of 10°＜2θ＜40°, indicating that the PoPD fibers are long-range ordered (PDF NO. 00-047-2143). The peak located at 28.9° corresponds to a d spacing of 0.31 nm, which agrees with the π-π stacking distance. In contrast, the fluorescent 1D PoPD shows markedly different crystals compared to the PoPD fibers. Green and red fibers display broad reflections due to amorphous components, while yellow fibers show enhanced intensity at a peak located at 31.3°, suggesting improved crystallinity. XRD data illustrates that after solution treatment, there is an obvious difference in the crystalline structure of the PoPD fluorescent fibers compared to before treatment. This change may be attributed to crystal structure recombination and surface defects.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD were recorded in Fig. 3 a. There are two absorption bands located at approximately 260 nm and 430 nm. The peaks observed around 260 nm are assigned to the π-π* transition of the CC bonds in aromatic compounds. The peaks observed around 430 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition associated with the phenazine unit conjugated to lone pairs of nitrogen atoms. While there is no significant change in the peaks around 260 nm, a blue-shift is observed in the peaks around 430 nm. This phenomenon could be mainly caused by surface functional groups and intermolecular forces.

The FT-IR spectrum was used to further confirm the molecular structure of the fluorescent 1D PoPD (Fig. 3 b). Prior to solution treatment, the PoPD fibers exhibited a typical phenazine-like structure, as evidenced by peaks observed at 1531 cm−1 corresponding to CC stretching vibrations in the benzene ring, 3171 cm−1 corresponding to N–H stretching vibrations in –NH2, and 1634 cm−1 corresponding to vibration peaks of CN in the phenazine ring. The red fluorescent fibers displayed a similar FT-IR spectrum to that of PoPD fibers, indicating identical molecular structures. In contrast, although the green and yellow fibers showed a similar shape in their FT-IR spectra, there were deviations (at 1530 cm−1) in the locations of characteristic peaks from their original positions. The fluorescence observed in green and yellow may be attributed to changes in molecular structure.

Ion resistance is an important property of fluorescent substances. We have studied the ion resistance of fluorescent 1D PoPD, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 c. The 1D PoPD exhibits good resistance to Li+, Na+, K+, Ca+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, NO3 −, NO2 −, AC−, SO4 2−. However, Hg2+ and H2O2 significantly quench the fluorescence of red, yellow, and green fibers. This may be due to the oxidation properties of Hg2+ and H2O2, which can result in changes in fluorescence functional groups leading to the quenching of fluorescence.

XPS surveys were employed to further confirm the electron distribution of PoPD fibers and fluorescent 1D PoPD. The results are shown in Fig. 4 and Figure S 4-7. Both polychromatic fibers and original fibers contain three elements: C, N, and O (Figure S 4). This is confirmed by the presence of three peaks located at approximately 284.8 eV (C 1s), 399.1 eV (N 1s), and 533.7 eV (O 1s).

In the high-resolution spectra of C 1s to o-PoPD, the spectrum shows two peaks at about 284.5 eV and 285.5 eV, which are ascribe to CC/C–C and C–N (Fig. 4 a). The N 1s spectrum shows three peaks at about 398.6 eV, 399.5 eV, and 401.2 eV, which are attributed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 4 b). The O 1s high-resolution spectra shows two peaks at about 534.5 eV and 532.5 eV, which can be assigned to C–O and CO (Fig. 4 c).

By comparing the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of fluorescent 1D PoPD with different fluorescent color, it was found that after treatment with NaOH solution, OH− changes the structure of carbon-containing functional groups, resulting in the formation of new bonds such as CN/CO and C–O as confirmed by additional peaks located at about 288.3 eV and 289.3 eV (Fig. 4 d). These variations are believed to be responsible for the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups through OH−, leading to green fluorescence. Yellow fluorescence is the result of the synergistic action of OH−/-OH, as evidenced by the presence of C–OH in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum. This is supported by the peak observed at approximately 282.9 eV (Fig. 4 e). Compared with o-PoPD, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum of r-PoPD, which was treated with –OH, only shows the addition of C–OH (Fig. 4 f).

Benefitting from the changes observed in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum, we discovered that treatment with OH− induced CN/CO and C–O type C, while treatment with –OH induced C–OH type C. Furthermore, treatment with both OH−/-OH resulted in the induction of CN/CO, C–O and C–OH type C. Based on this conclusion; we can calculate that the presence of various functional groups containing carbon ultimately leads to a shift in the wavelength of fluorescence emission.

The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of fibers is composed of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N (Figure S 5 a, Figure S 6 a, Figure S 7 a). Compared with the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of o-PoPD, multicolor fluorescent fibers exhibit C–OH/N–OH type O (Figure S 5 b, Figure S 6 b, Figure S 7 b). The percentage of functional groups in the fibers is shown in Fig. 4 g. The proportion of oxygen-containing functional groups in the fibers treated with OH− or –OH significantly increased. Compared with o-PoPD and r-PoPD, the CN/CO and C–O group contents of g-PoPD and y-PoPD are increased, indicating that deprotonation in g-PoPD and y-PoPD. Furthermore, compared to o-PoPD and g-PoPD, the content of C–OH group is increased in y-PoPD and r-PoPD, suggesting that hydrogen bonding is prevalent in y-PoPD and r-PoPD. Therefore, we can infer that deprotonation is the main cause of the blue-shift in emission wavelength as evidenced by the green fluorescence emitted by g-PoPD and the increase in CN/CO and C–O group content. Correspondingly, hydrogen bonding is considered to be responsible for red-shift in emission wavelength as demonstrated by the red fluorescence emitted by r-PoPD along with the increase in C–OH group content. The percentages of C, N, O, N/C and O/C atomic ratios in the fibers are consistent with this conclusion (Fig. 4 h).

Fig. 5 illustrates the probable mechanism of multicolor fluorescent 1D PoPD. We attribute this modulation of fluorescence emission wavelength of multicolor fluorescent 1D PoPD to the OH−/-OH synergistic effect. Firstly, oPD monomers were chemically oxidized and transformed into the non-fluorescent 2,3-phenazine like

structure. However, the 2,3-phenazine like structure underwent a corresponding change in the next post-treatment process. OH− in OH−/-OH synergistic effect provides alkaline environment, 2,3-phenazine like structure undergoes deprotonation process, and –NH2 loses proton and thus becomes negatively charged, which has a significant effect on the electron delocalization state in conjugated π-bonds, increases its photon hopping band gap, thus generation of new green fluorescent chromophores. –OH in OH−/-OH synergistic effect provides hydroxyl group with pronounced hydrogen bonding effect. For 2,3-phenazine like structure, strong hydrogen bonds can be formed between –NH2 and –OH, which leads to a solvation effect due to hydrogen bonding, reducing the luminescence energy band gap, which in turn leads to a red-shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength. When OH− and –OH are co-presented, the fluorescence blue shift due to the deprotonation effect induced by OH− and the fluorescence red shift due to hydrogen bonding produced by –OH form an equilibrium situation, and as a result, a yellow fluorescence in the intermediate wavelength band is produced.

To further demonstrate the potential application of multicolor LEDs, we fabricated g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD fibers to UV chips LEDs. As expected, when stimulated by the UV chips, g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD fibers LEDs emitted green, yellow and red light in the dark field (Fig. 6 a-c), with the CIE color coordinates (Fig. 6 i) of (0.37, 0.54), (0.44, 0.47), (0.55, 0.41), respectively. The emission spectra shown in Fig. 6 e-g are consistent with the CIE color coordinates. To achieve white light, we fabricated g-PoPD onto bule chips resulting in typical white light as seen in Fig. 6 d, with CIE color coordinates of (0.28, 0.27) shown in Fig. 6 i. The emission spectra of white LEDs are displayed in Fig. 6 h, which align well with the CIE color coordinates. The successful application of PoPD multicolor fluorescent fibers in LEDs once again demonstrates the feasibility of regulating the emission wavelength of aromatic amine fluorescent materials through OH−/-OH synergistic action.

In summary, we demonstrated a novel synergistic OH−/-OH method to control the fluorescent properties of materials and achieved green, yellow and red fluorescent 1D PoPD. Firstly, we obtained high purity of PoPD fibers through chemical oxidation methods using H2O2 as oxidizing agent, thereby avoiding interference from heavy ions. Furthermore, synergistic effect of OH−/-OH not only achieves regulation of the fluorescent emission wavelength, but also alters the functional group and structure of PoPD. Structurally, the synergistic OH−/-OH treatment resulting in numerous surface defects, transforms the solid fibers to hollow tubes. Additionally, nonfluorescent o-PoPD fibers emit multicolor green, yellow and red fluorescence after treated with a OH−/-OH solution. Based on the experimental results, we attributed the multicolor fluorescent behavior to the synergistic action of OH−/-OH. The deprotonation process of OH− caused blue-shift in fluorescence emission wavelength, resulting in green fluorescence fiber. The formation of a hydrogen bond between –OH and –NH2 was responsible for the red fluorescence fiber. By combining the effects of blue-shift and red-shift, the synergistic action of OH−/-OH achieved middle wavelength emission in yellow fluorescence fiber. Finally, we fabricated g-PoPD, y-PoPD and r-PoPD fibers onto UV LEDs chips and obtained green, yellow and red light in the dark field. White LEDs were achieved by using g-PoPD with blue chips. This work introduces a new OH−/-OH synergistic strategy to control the emission wavelength of aromatic amine materials, thereby expanding the research on multicolored fluorescence fibers.

Kunpeng Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yiran Bai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Investigation. Guisheng Zhu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Huarui Xu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Tingting Wei: Investigation. Yunyun Zhao: Resources. Fei Shang: Writing – review & editing. Chunyu Xu: Investigation. Li Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation. Lili Han: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Science and Technology Base and Talent Special Project of Guangxi Province (Grant No. AD21075022, AD21220029); Middle-aged and Young Teachers' Basic Ability Promotion Project of Guangxi (Grant No. 2021KY0215); Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials Foundation (Grant No. 211016-Z); Research Foundation of Guilin University of Technology (Grant No. GUTQDJJ2019011); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. U21A2065); Science and Technology Major Project of Guangxi (Grant No. AA21077018, AB20297053).
